Hotel Strategies

Hejun Chen

2022-12-15

R packages

library(tidyverse)
library(magrittr) 
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(readr)
library(pander)
theme_set(theme_bw())

Main Question: What strategies can the hotel implement to increase revenues?

Get the data

# read the data and show the first five rows
df <- read_csv("hotel_bookings.csv")
df %>% head(5)
## # A tibble: 5 × 32
##   hotel        is_canceled lead_time arrival_date_ye… arrival_date_mo… arrival_date_we…
##   <chr>              <dbl>     <dbl>            <dbl> <chr>                       <dbl>
## 1 Resort Hotel           0       342             2015 July                           27
## 2 Resort Hotel           0       737             2015 July                           27
## 3 Resort Hotel           0         7             2015 July                           27
## 4 Resort Hotel           0        13             2015 July                           27
## 5 Resort Hotel           0        14             2015 July                           27
## # … with 26 more variables: arrival_date_day_of_month <dbl>,
## #   stays_in_weekend_nights <dbl>, stays_in_week_nights <dbl>, adults <dbl>,
## #   children <dbl>, babies <dbl>, meal <chr>, country <chr>,
## #   market_segment <chr>, distribution_channel <chr>, is_repeated_guest <dbl>,
## #   previous_cancellations <dbl>, previous_bookings_not_canceled <dbl>,
## #   reserved_room_type <chr>, assigned_room_type <chr>, booking_changes <dbl>,
## #   deposit_type <chr>, agent <chr>, company <chr>, …

Hotel Type:

City hotels are more popular than resort hotels

# Create barplots to compare sales through different hotel types
df %>% count(hotel) %>% ggplot() + 
  aes(x = hotel, y = n, fill = hotel) + 
  geom_bar(stat = 'identity') +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "Hotel Type",
       y = "Count",
       title = "Booking Count by Hotel Type",
       fill = "Hotel Type")

Cancellation rate:

Overall cancelation rate is near 40%, which is very high

# Replace numeric indicators with specific string values
df$is_canceled[df$is_canceled == 0] <- "Not Cancelled"
df$is_canceled[df$is_canceled == 1] <- "Cancelled"
cancel <- df %>% 
        group_by(is_canceled) %>% 
         tally() %>% 
          mutate(perc = n / sum(n))

# Plot a pie chart to visualization cancellation rate
ggplot(cancel, aes(x = "", y = perc, fill = is_canceled)) +
  geom_bar(width = 1, stat = "identity", color = 'white') +
  coord_polar("y", start=0) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank()) +
  geom_text(aes(x = 1.6, label = scales::percent(perc, accuracy = .1)), 
            position = position_stack(vjust = .5)) + 
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Cancellation Pie Chart",
       fill = "Booking Status")

Bookings by country:

We can see that a large proportion (~40%) of bookings are from Portugal, and then Great Britain(10%).

# Create a table to show percentage of bookings by country
df %>% count(country) %>% 
  mutate(percentage = round(100 * n / sum(n),1)) %>% 
  arrange(desc(percentage)) %>% 
  head(5) %>% 
  pander
country n percentage
PRT 48590 40.7
GBR 12129 10.2
FRA 10415 8.7
ESP 8568 7.2
DEU 7287 6.1

Bookings over time:

Hotel bookings are low during cold months around November ~ February. The bookings nearly double around April to October.

# Create a table to show percentage of bookings by month
df %>% count(arrival_date_month) %>% 
  mutate(percentage = round(100 * n / sum(n),1)) %>% 
  arrange(desc(percentage)) %>% 
  pander
arrival_date_month n percentage
August 13877 11.6
July 12661 10.6
May 11791 9.9
April 11089 9.3
October 11160 9.3
June 10939 9.2
September 10508 8.8
March 9794 8.2
February 8068 6.8
December 6780 5.7
November 6794 5.7
January 5929 5

Bookings by market segment:

Majority of the bookings were made through Online & Offline TA

# Create a table to show percentage of bookings by market segment
df %>% count(market_segment) %>% 
  mutate(percentage = round(100 * n / sum(n),1)) %>% 
  arrange(desc(percentage)) %>% 
  head(5) %>% 
  pander
market_segment n percentage
Online TA 56477 47.3
Offline TA/TO 24219 20.3
Groups 19811 16.6
Direct 12606 10.6
Corporate 5295 4.4

Cancellation with lead time:

Customers with a lead_time > 200 days barely actually checked out

# Create a table to show the count of each reservation_status and lead_time pair 
df_leadtime <- df %>% 
  group_by(reservation_status, lead_time) %>% 
  summarise(total_count = n())

# Plot line plot to show trends of different reservation_status over lead_time
df_leadtime %>% 
  ggplot() + 
  aes(x = lead_time, y = total_count, color = reservation_status) +
  geom_line() + 
  facet_wrap(. ~ reservation_status, scales = "free_y") + 
  theme_bw()

Cancellation by market segment:

Bookings made through groups have the highest cancelation rate

# Create a table to show the count of each reservation_status and market_segment pair 
df_market_segment <- df %>% 
  group_by(market_segment, reservation_status) %>% 
  summarise(total_count = n())

# Calculate the cancellation rate within each market_segment
df_market_segment %>% 
  group_by(market_segment) %>% 
  summarise(reservation_status = reservation_status, total_count = total_count, cancel_percentage = total_count/sum(total_count)) %>% 
  filter(reservation_status == "Canceled") %>% 
  arrange(desc(cancel_percentage)) %>% 
  select(-c("reservation_status")) %>% 
  head(5) %>% 
  pander

summarise() has grouped output by ‘market_segment’. You can override using the .groups argument.

market_segment total_count cancel_percentage
Undefined 2 1
Groups 12023 0.6069
Online TA 20148 0.3567
Offline TA/TO 8080 0.3336
Aviation 41 0.173

Customer Loyalty:

Most customers won’t come back after their first purchase

# Replace numeric values with specific string values for easier visualization
df$is_repeated_guest[df$is_repeated_guest == 0] <- "No"
df$is_repeated_guest[df$is_repeated_guest == 1] <- "Yes"

# Plot barplots to show numbers of repeated vs. non-repeated customers
df %>% count(is_repeated_guest) %>% 
  ggplot() + 
  aes(x = is_repeated_guest, y = n, fill = is_repeated_guest) + 
  geom_bar(stat = 'identity')

Conclusion

Since we want to increase revenues for the hotels, we should develop strategies to conduct most effective marketing and overbooking, as well as to recover loss from booking cancellations.

First, we can invest more money in marketing in Portugal because of its high customer base (~40%), and countries with relatively high customer bases like the Great Britain (~10%) to increase our market shares.

Second, we can do more marketing during popular months (April~ October) because people travel more during these months. In contrast, we can lower hotel costs (hiring less employees) during cold months (November ~ March) to save money.

Third, since majority of the bookings were made through Online and offline tech assistants, we should train those assistants well so they can provide better/more effective services to our potential customers in the booking process.

Fourth, we see that hotels suffer from high cancellation rates. Since customers with a lead time that is more than 200 days barely check-in, we can do overbooking (which means we assume the customers won’t arrive and still sell the booked rooms to new customers.) Also, we see that ~60% of bookings made as groups were cancelled. So, to recover the loss, we can charge a higher cancellation fee if the booking is made by groups.

Fifth, we see that we have a very low number of repeated customers, which indicates that we don’t have a good customer loyalty strategy. We should develop strategies like follow-up marketing emails, bonuses for returning customers, etc. to attract our old customers and thus gain a larger market growth.
